name: Russell's Valence-Arousal Circumplex
core_claim: Russell's circumplex model of affect (1980) claims that all affective states can be located in a single two-dimensional space defined by two orthogonal, continuous neurophysiological dimensions: valence (a pleasure-displeasure continuum) and arousal (an activation/alertness continuum, from sleepy/calm to excited/energised). Discrete emotion words (happy, calm, sad, tense, etc.) are not primitives but emergent points arranged in a circle around the perimeter of this space — hence 'circumplex'. The dominant claim for media work is therefore that the affective character of an experience, and the affective need a viewer is trying to satisfy, reduce to a position in V-A space rather than to a list of named emotions or content topics. For recommendation, the model says the affective dimension that matters most is the (valence, arousal) coordinate of the felt experience, and that mood-fit is a distance computation in that 2D plane.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Purely experiential/affective, and at a very abstract level. The circumplex says nothing about what a story is ABOUT — it has no topical, structural, motivational, or values content whatsoever. It captures only the felt affective quality (how pleasant, how activating) of an emotional state. Applied to media it describes the affective texture of the experience (or the mood a viewer is in / wants to be in), not its subject matter. It is even more content-agnostic than most affective frameworks: where 'tone' might distinguish bleak from warm, the circumplex collapses every distinction into two scalar coordinates. It is a dimensional model of feeling, deliberately stripped of semantic/narrative information.
key_dimensions: Two orthogonal bipolar axes: (1) VALENCE / pleasure — displeasure (horizontal, negative to positive); (2) AROUSAL / activation — sleepy/calm to alert/excited (vertical, low to high). These define four quadrants commonly used in applied work: high-arousal positive (excited, elated, happy, alert), high-arousal negative (tense, angry, afraid, frustrated, stressed), low-arousal negative (sad, depressed, bored, fatigued, gloomy), low-arousal positive (calm, relaxed, content, serene, at ease). Around the perimeter, the 28 affect terms Russell mapped fall at characteristic angles (e.g. happy ~ upper-right, tense ~ upper-left, sad/depressed ~ lower-left, relaxed ~ lower-right). Some treatments add a weak third dimension (dominance/potency, the 'D' in the related PAD model of Mehrabian & Russell), but the canonical circumplex is strictly 2D. In applied media/music systems the work item or the song is given a (valence, arousal) score, often by clustering features into the four quadrants.
cross_cultural_validity: Comparatively well-validated cross-culturally relative to most frameworks in this taxonomy, but not pancultural. Russell himself ran cross-cultural studies (Russell, Lewicka & Niit, 1989) showing the 2D circumplex structure recovered from similarity judgments of emotion words across Estonian, Greek, and Polish speakers and from facial expressions among Greek and Chinese speakers; subsequent work replicated the two-dimensional valence/arousal structure in many languages and with facial-expression stimuli. The structure (two dimensions, circular arrangement) is robust across the cultures tested. IMPORTANT caveat repeatedly noted by Russell and others: the model cannot be called pancultural because too few cultures have been tested, and ethnographic work suggests some South Pacific cultures (e.g. Ifaluk, Samoan) may not organise feelings along an arousal dimension at all, and may emphasise relational/moral framings of emotion that the dimensions miss. So: strong evidence for broad (especially Western/literate/urban) generality of the 2D structure; genuine open question at the cultural margins, particularly regarding the universality of arousal as a salient dimension.
empirical_grounding: The DIMENSIONAL STRUCTURE itself is among the most empirically robust constructs in affective science: derived by Russell (1980) via multidimensional scaling and factor-analytic clustering of similarity judgments on 28 emotion words, and corroborated by decades of self-report, facial-expression, and (later) affective-neuroscience work (Posner, Russell & Peterson, 2005 link valence and arousal to dissociable neural systems). However, grounding AGAINST MEDIA ENJOYMENT/COMPLETION/PREFERENCE is much weaker and mostly computational/correlational rather than experimental: music-emotion-recognition (MER) and affective music/video recommenders routinely score items by (valence, arousal) and cluster into quadrants (e.g. k-means on the AV plane), and report retrieval/classification accuracy, but few studies causally test that minimising V-A distance to a target mood increases ENJOYMENT or completion. Music has far more V-A annotation data (DEAM, AMG1608, Emotify, etc.) than film/TV; for narrative TV/anime, item-level V-A is typically inferred, not measured. Critically, mood-fit is an INTERACTION (item V-A x viewer's current/desired V-A), so item V-A scores alone cannot validate the framework — you need per-session viewer mood data plus realised choices/satisfaction. To validate LLM-extracted V-A against this framework you would need: (a) human V-A ratings of a title set as ground truth, (b) inter-rater reliability on those, and (c) ideally downstream enjoyment data conditioned on viewer state.
operationalisability: Partially and coarsely extractable. An LLM can plausibly place a whole work on the two axes from a synopsis + genre + tone signals: valence (overall pleasant/uplifting vs grim/distressing tone) and arousal (energetic/intense/fast vs calm/slow/contemplative) are both inferable at low resolution. This is one of the more extractable affective frameworks precisely because it asks for only two scalars. BUT it is genuinely hard at finer resolution and prone to systematic error. Extraction unit is ambiguous and matters: a single whole-work (V, A) coordinate is a poor summary of works whose entire appeal is affective TRAJECTORY (a tragedy moves from high to low valence; a thriller oscillates arousal; comedy-drama mixes quadrants). Arc- or scene-level extraction is more faithful but far less reliable from a synopsis, which rarely encodes pacing or emotional dynamics. Failure modes: (1) conflating subject-matter valence with experiential valence (a film ABOUT grief can be a warm, life-affirming watch — low subject valence, positive felt valence); (2) reading dark themes as low arousal when they are tense/high arousal; (3) genre-label anchoring (labelling all horror high-arousal-negative, ignoring slow folk-horror dread which is low-arousal); (4) ignoring that the same work occupies different coordinates for different viewers; (5) flattening bittersweet/poignant works that deliberately sit at mixed/ambiguous valence, which the bipolar valence axis cannot represent well.
motivation_served: Primarily hedonic and mood-regulatory: the circumplex is the affective substrate beneath mood-management and selective-exposure accounts (it supplies the V-A coordinates those theories regulate toward). It maps cleanly to mood repair (move valence up), arousal regulation (calm the over-aroused, energise the bored), and arousal/valence maintenance. It maps POORLY to eudaimonic appreciation — being moved, meaning-making, and the enjoyment of sad or tragic content are exactly the counter-hedonic cases a simple 'maximise valence' reading mispredicts (poignancy lives at low/mixed valence yet is appreciated). For GROUP viewing it is unusually tractable because two people's current and desired moods are both points in the same 2D space: a joint pick can be modelled as finding a title whose (V, A) minimises some aggregate distance to both partners' target coordinates, or that lands in a quadrant tolerable to both. The arousal axis is often the sharper group constraint (a stressed/over-aroused partner and a bored/under-aroused partner want opposite arousal), suggesting the recommender solicit a quick per-session mood reading from each viewer and trade off in V-A space.
receiver_dependence: Mixed, and the distinction is central for a two-person recommender. The item's (valence, arousal) coordinate is treated as a near-ABSOLUTE work property in applied systems (a song or scene has a V-A score), and at a coarse level valence/arousal of a work are reasonably consensual — but they are softer than a truly physical attribute like gore level, because felt valence depends on the viewer's appraisal (one viewer finds a tense thriller exhilarating/positive-arousal, another finds it stressful/negative). The DESIRABILITY of any coordinate is wholly RELATIONAL: it depends on the viewer's current mood and the mood they want, which are per-session, per-person inputs not stored in a taste profile. So the framework yields one quasi-absolute item axis pair (item V-A) plus a purely relational utility (distance from the item's coordinate to each viewer's transient target coordinate). For HelpME2C this argues for storing a stable per-title (V, A) estimate separately from a transient per-session, per-person mood/target-mood input layer, and computing fit as a distance in that shared space.
blind_spots: Blind to essentially all content and meaning. Two works with identical (V, A) coordinates can differ completely in subject, theme, moral substance, and genre — the circumplex cannot tell a tense war drama from a tense heist or a tense horror, so it is orthogonal to (and useless as a substitute for) a thematic taxonomy. It cannot represent MIXED or AMBIGUOUS valence well (bittersweet, poignant, tragicomic states sit awkwardly on a single bipolar pleasant-unpleasant axis; some researchers argue valence is not truly bipolar and positive/negative affect can co-occur). It collapses qualitatively distinct emotions that share coordinates (anger and fear both high-arousal-negative; awe and excitement both high-arousal-positive) — losing distinctions that matter for appeal. It ignores the DYNAMICS that often constitute enjoyment (arc, build, release, catharsis) by summarising a temporal experience as a static point. It says nothing about eudaimonic/meaning motivations, social context, identity, or narrative craft. Its arousal dimension may not be cross-culturally universal. And by design it has no dominance/agency dimension (the PAD model adds it back), so it cannot distinguish, e.g., fear (low dominance) from anger (high dominance) within the high-arousal-negative quadrant.
vocabulary_implications: The circumplex does NOT supply thematic content slugs and must not be folded into the theme taxonomy — its two scalars are orthogonal to content and belong in a SEPARATE, lightweight per-title affective layer, mirroring the recommendation already made for Mood Management. Recommended encoding (consistent across both frameworks so they share one layer): add two title-level affective dimensions extractable from synopsis+genre+tone — (1) valence (negative / mixed / positive) capturing overall felt emotional tone, explicitly distinct from subject-matter darkness; (2) arousal (low / medium / high) capturing energy/intensity/pacing. These align directly with Mood Management's hedonic_valence and excitatory_potential, so the two studies converge on the SAME two axes — adopt one shared pair rather than duplicating. Add an explicit 'mixed' / 'bittersweet' value on valence (the circumplex's bipolarity is a known weakness; poignant works need a non-binary slot). Flag as NOT reliably extractable from synopsis alone: (a) affective TRAJECTORY / arc-level V-A (needs scene/structure or engagement data) — store at most a coarse whole-work coordinate and note the loss; (b) viewer's current and desired mood (must be solicited per session, per person); (c) the dominance/agency dimension (PAD) if ever needed — not in synopsis. Product hook: a quick per-session two-axis mood input (how each viewer feels / wants to feel) enabling a V-A distance computation for the joint pick.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Orthogonal to the content slugs but a clean formal backbone for the texture/tone layer — it gives the implicit affective register of several existing slugs an explicit 2-coordinate home. It VALIDATES the tonal/texture slugs that already encode V-A information: slow-burn and meditation-on-time/meditation-on-death imply LOW arousal; absurdist-comedy and dark-comedy imply positive-leaning valence delivered through humour despite grim subject (a key V-A insight: felt valence != subject valence); existential-horror/cosmic-horror/folk-horror imply negative valence but split on arousal (cosmic/folk dread is often LOW-arousal unease, jump-scare horror HIGH-arousal) — a distinction the circumplex sharpens and the current single-bucket 'horror' texture set blurs. It suggests these texture slugs are MISALIGNED only insofar as they leave V-A implicit and inconsistent; the fix is to map each to explicit valence/arousal values rather than relabel them. The clear GAP it reveals (identical to the Mood Management finding, reinforcing it): the vocabulary has no first-class, content-independent valence axis, no arousal/energy axis, no representation of mixed/bittersweet valence, and no transient per-session viewer-mood mechanism. It does NOT motivate adding or removing any content theme slug (found-family, power-corrupts, etc. are out of scope); it operates entirely in the affective layer beside them.
key_sources: Russell, J. A. (1980) 'A Circumplex Model of Affect', Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 39(6):1161-1178 [seminal; paywalled, PDF on ResearchGate https://www.researchgate.net/publication/235361517_A_circumplex_model_of_affect — free]. Russell, J. A., Lewicka, M. & Niit, T. (1989) 'A Cross-Cultural Study of a Circumplex Model of Affect', Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 57(5):848-856 [PDF free: https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/a43d/6226263f2e7b039c4551601c51867ae9530c.pdf]. Posner, J., Russell, J. A. & Peterson, B. S. (2005) 'The circumplex model of affect: an integrative approach to affective neuroscience, cognitive development, and psychopathology', Development and Psychopathology 17(3):715-734 [free PMC: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2367156/]. Mehrabian & Russell (1974) PAD (pleasure-arousal-dominance) model [book; paywalled]. Applied/recommender: 'Music mood classification model based on arousal-valence values' IEEE (https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/5745796/) [paywalled]; 'Induced Emotion-Based Music Recommendation through Reinforcement Learning', Applied Sciences MDPI (https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/12/21/11209) [free]; 'Words to Waves: Emotion-Adaptive Music Recommendation System', arXiv (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.21724) [free]. Overview: MorphCast (https://www.morphcast.com/blog/circumplex-model-of-affects/) [free]; PSU open textbook (https://psu.pb.unizin.org/psych425/chapter/circumplex-models/) [free].
